vitamin_a.mainFunction: Maintains vision, skin and mucosal integrity, immune function
vitamin_a.specialNotes: Generally not deficient; avoid long-term high-dose supplementation
vitamin_a.supplementAdvice: Omnivores typically don't need extra supplementation. Pregnant women should follow medical advice and not exceed the UL (3000μg RAE). Night blindness or severe deficiency requires medically supervised supplementation.

vitamin_c.mainFunction: Antioxidant, promotes collagen synthesis, enhances immunity
vitamin_c.supplementAdvice: Those with a balanced diet don't need extra supplementation. Smokers, high-stress individuals, and those prone to colds may consider 100–200mg daily. High doses (>1000mg) may cause diarrhea.

vitamin_d.mainFunction: Promotes calcium absorption, bone health, immune regulation
vitamin_d.specialNotes: Deficiency is extremely common; testing 25(OH)D levels is recommended
vitamin_d.supplementAdvice: ⚠️ Most people with insufficient sun exposure need extra supplementation. Recommended: 400–2000 IU daily. Winter, high latitudes, dark skin, and indoor workers must supplement — food sources are nearly insufficient.

vitamin_e.supplementAdvice: A typical diet with nuts and vegetable oils is usually sufficient. High doses (>400 IU) may increase bleeding risk, especially in those on anticoagulants.

vitamin_k.overdoseRisk: Large doses of synthetic K3 may cause hemolysis
vitamin_k.supplementAdvice: A diet rich in leafy greens is usually sufficient. Newborns require K1 injection to prevent bleeding. Those at high risk of osteoporosis may consider K2 (MK-7, 90–180μg).

vitamin_b1.supplementAdvice: A typical diet with whole grains and lean meat is usually sufficient. Heavy drinkers and those who eat mainly refined grains should pay attention to B1 intake.

vitamin_b2.supplementAdvice: A typical diet is usually sufficient. Vegetarians, pregnant women, and picky-eating children should pay attention. B2 is usually included in B-complex supplements.

vitamin_b3.mainFunction: Energy metabolism, skin and nerve health
vitamin_b3.supplementAdvice: A typical diet is usually sufficient. Pellagra patients require therapeutic doses under medical supervision. High doses (>35mg) may cause skin flushing; extended-release forms reduce this.

vitamin_b6.mainFunction: Amino acid metabolism, neurotransmitter synthesis, hematopoiesis
vitamin_b6.specialNotes: Can relieve morning sickness; B6 + magnesium work synergistically
vitamin_b6.supplementAdvice: Omnivores generally don't need extra supplementation. Those with morning sickness may consider B6 + ginger (10–25mg/day). Oral contraceptive users may have increased needs.

vitamin_b12.specialNotes: Found almost exclusively in animal products; strict vegetarians must supplement
vitamin_b12.supplementAdvice: ⚠️ Strict vegetarians must supplement (25–100μg/day). Older adults (>50) have reduced stomach acid and poor absorption; supplementation is recommended. Gastrectomy patients may need injections.

folate.mainFunction: DNA synthesis, prevention of fetal neural tube defects
folate.specialNotes: Supplement 400–800μg daily from pre-conception through early pregnancy
folate.supplementAdvice: ⚠️ Women trying to conceive and those in early pregnancy must supplement (400–800μg DFE/day) to prevent neural tube defects. Cooking destroys 50–90%; supplements have better absorption.
atRiskGroups: Women trying to conceive and pregnant women

biotin.mainFunction: Fat and glucose metabolism, skin and hair health
biotin.supplementAdvice: A typical diet (eggs, liver, nuts) is usually sufficient. Those who eat raw egg whites (which contain avidin) or those on long-term parenteral nutrition should pay attention.

pantothenic_acid.supplementAdvice: Found in almost all foods; deficiency is extremely rare. No additional supplementation needed.

calcium.mainFunction: Bones and teeth, neuromuscular transmission, blood clotting
calcium.supplementAdvice: Those who regularly consume dairy and soy products don't need extra supplementation. ⚠️ Those with insufficient dairy intake (teens, pregnant women, elderly, vegetarians) should supplement with calcium citrate 500–600mg/day, divided doses, with Vitamin D.

iron.specialNotes: Heme iron (animal) is far more absorbable than plant iron; Vitamin C enhances iron absorption
iron.supplementAdvice: ⚠️ Menstruating women, pregnant women, and vegetarians are prone to deficiency. Those diagnosed with iron-deficiency anemia need 20–30mg elemental iron/day (under medical supervision). Test serum ferritin before supplementing.

zinc.supplementAdvice: ⚠️ Vegetarians have low absorption rates and should pay attention. Omnivores generally don't need extra supplementation. Supplements: low dose recommended (8–11mg/day).

magnesium.mainFunction: Neuromuscular function, heart rhythm, bone health, blood sugar regulation
magnesium.overdoseRisk: Diarrhea, low blood pressure, respiratory depression
magnesium.specialNotes: Magnesium glycinate/citrate absorbs well; magnesium oxide causes diarrhea
magnesium.supplementAdvice: High-stress individuals, diabetics, and heavy drinkers should pay attention. Choose magnesium glycinate or citrate (better absorption); avoid magnesium oxide (causes diarrhea).

selenium.overdoseRisk: Hair loss, nail deformity, garlic-like breath
selenium.specialNotes: 1–2 Brazil nuts per day can meet daily requirements
selenium.supplementAdvice: Residents in selenium-poor regions should pay attention. 1–2 Brazil nuts per day is usually sufficient. Very high doses (>400μg) are toxic.

iodine.specialNotes: Hashimoto's patients should be cautious with iodine; avoid high-iodine foods
iodine.supplementAdvice: Those using iodized salt are typically sufficient. ⚠️ Pregnant and breastfeeding women need 200–250μg/day. Supplements require medical guidance; excess causes thyroid dysfunction.

potassium.supplementAdvice: A typical diet with vegetables and fruits is usually sufficient. Those with kidney disease should avoid high-potassium supplements.

omega3.specialNotes: Vegetarians can use algae DHA as an alternative
omega3.supplementAdvice: ⚠️ Those who rarely eat deep-sea fish (<2 times/week) should supplement with fish oil or algae DHA 200–300mg/day. Strongly recommended for pregnant women and those at high cardiovascular risk.

fiber.mainFunction: Gut health, blood sugar and lipid control
fiber.specialNotes: IBS-D patients should prefer soluble fiber; IBS-C should increase insoluble fiber
fiber.supplementAdvice: Those with constipation, obesity, diabetes, and hyperlipidemia should increase to 35g/day. Increase gradually with plenty of water to avoid bloating.

protein.mainFunction: Tissue repair, enzyme and hormone synthesis
protein.overdoseRisk: Kidney damage (in those with pre-existing kidney disease)
protein.specialNotes: 1.6–2.2g/kg body weight recommended during muscle-building phase
protein.supplementAdvice: A typical diet is usually sufficient. Those in muscle-building phase, post-surgery, elderly, and vegetarians should pay attention to quality protein intake. Protein powders (whey/pea/rice) can assist but are not essential.
